title: Dye Transfer on Linen
timeEstimate: 1–6 hour cool soak; speed-critical, heat sets transfer permanently
stainChemistry: Dye transfer is bleed from one garment onto another during washing — usually dark denim, new colored items, red or navy goods, or non-colorfast fabric. The migrating dye bonds to linen's cellulose fibers through molecular grip, the same mechanism as intentional dyeing. Once heat-set (hot wash or hot dryer) the bond is essentially permanent. Linen is more delicate than cotton — it wrinkles aggressively when wet and tolerates less mechanical agitation. White linen tolerates oxygen bleach and reducing-bleach approaches. Dyed linen is more constrained because color removers strip carpet/garment dye too. The single hard line: NEVER chlorine bleach on dye transfer — chlorine can lock azo dyes into a yellow oxidation product that is permanent. Heat is the second hard line.
whyThisWorks: Speed plus oxygen bleach on white linen is the most reliable home approach: re-wash IMMEDIATELY in cool water before the dye bonds, then OxiClean cool soak to break color bonds. For stubborn or partially set transfer on white linen, a reducing-bleach color remover (Carbona Color Run Remover or Rit Color Remover) is the home nuclear option — but it strips ALL dye, so it's whites-only. For dyed linen, options narrow sharply — pro is often the right call because the bench is shorter. The recurring trap: chlorine bleach. People reach for it instinctively and it makes the stain permanent.
defaultAssumption: White or light linen garment / napkin / tablecloth + dye transfer from a dark item in the wash, fresh and not yet hot-dried. Hot-dried = often permanent. Dyed / structured / vintage / dry-clean-only linen = pro.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Has it been hot-dried? If yes, often permanent — escalate. Care label dry-clean-only / structured / vintage = pro. Otherwise: act fast, don't dry the item again until clear.

spottingProtocol.technique: Repeat the wash before dye sets further
spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, cool water, normal detergent
spottingProtocol.instruction: Re-wash the item in cool water with normal detergent IMMEDIATELY. Do NOT put it in the dryer. The dye is still partly soluble in cool water within the first wash — speed is everything.

spottingProtocol.technique: Oxygen bleach to break color bonds
spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean per label, cool water, soak basin
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix OxiClean per label in COOL water. Soak the linen 1–6 hours. Check every hour — pull when transfer clears. Cool rinse, cool re-wash. Repeat soak if partial improvement.

spottingProtocol.agent: Color Remover for White Linen Only — Nuclear Option
spottingProtocol.equipment: Carbona Color Run Remover or Rit Color Remover, ventilation
spottingProtocol.instruction: WHITE linen only. Per label — Carbona Color Run Remover or Rit Color Remover (sodium hydrosulfite). Strips ALL dye, including any colored carpet dye it lands on. Ventilate (sulfur smell). Cool rinse, cool wash.

spottingProtocol.agent: 3% Hydrogen Peroxide Spot Pass for Residual Yellow
spottingProtocol.technique: Oxidize residual color on white linen
spottingProtocol.equipment: 3% H₂O₂ on damp cloth or as soak additive
spottingProtocol.instruction: WHITE linen only — hidden test on dyed linen first. 3% peroxide damp cloth or as add to OxiClean soak. Cool rinse.
AIR-DRY in direct sunlight. UV photodegrades residual dye. Inspect dry — if clear, normal wash going forward. If color remains, NEVER hot dryer / iron until cleared — heat permanently sets dye.

steps: 1. Triage — hot-dried (often permanent) vs fresh, structured / vintage = pro.
3. OxiClean cool soak 1–6 hr, check hourly.
4. Color Remover (reducing bleach) for white linen only, ventilation.
5. 3% H₂O₂ spot for residual yellow on white linen.
6. Sun-dry plus air-dry inspection. NO heat until clear.
products: Carbona Color Run Remover or Rit Color Remover (white linen only)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat permanently sets dye transfer
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach — locks dye into permanent yellow oxidation
NEVER hot water or hot dryer — sets dye transfer permanently
Color Remover is white-only — strips all dye including the linen's
Speed matters more than chemistry choice
Sulfur smell from reducing bleach — ventilate

homeSolutions.agent: Speed Beats Chemistry — Don't Dry It
homeSolutions.instruction: Dye transfer is recoverable when fresh. Once hot-dried, often permanent. Two hard rules: (1) NO chlorine bleach (locks the dye into a yellow oxidation product permanently — instinctive and wrong) and (2) NO heat until the transfer is fully cleared. The order: act fast, cool re-wash, oxygen bleach soak, then escalate.

Dry-clean-only linen, structured pieces (lined jackets, blazers), vintage linen, or dyed colored linen all narrow your home options sharply. White or light washable linen = home protocol candidate. Anything else = pro.
Run the linen through a cool water cycle with normal detergent right now. Do NOT dry it. The dye is still partly soluble in cool water for the first wash — every hour you delay reduces success.

Mix OxiClean per label in COOL water in a basin or sink. Submerge the linen. Check every hour. Many transfers clear within 2 to 3 hours. Cool rinse, then a second cool wash with detergent. Repeat soak if it's most of the way there.

homeSolutions.agent: Color Remover — White Linen Only, Last Resort
homeSolutions.instruction: If white linen still has color after the OxiClean soak: Carbona Color Run Remover or Rit Color Remover (sodium hydrosulfite). Per label. Ventilate (sulfur smell). DO NOT use on colored / dyed linen — these strip ALL dye, including the linen's. Skip if you can't confirm white.

homeSolutions.agent: 3% Peroxide for Residual Yellow on White Linen
homeSolutions.instruction: Residual yellow shadow on WHITE linen: 3% drugstore peroxide on a damp cloth, or add to the OxiClean soak. Hidden test on dyed linen first.

homeSolutions.agent: Sun-Dry Plus AIR-DRY ONLY — No Heat Until Clear
homeSolutions.instruction: Hang in direct sunlight to dry. UV photodegrades residual dye. NEVER hot dryer or iron until the transfer is fully gone — heat permanently sets it. If color remains after sun-dry, repeat OxiClean soak before any heat.

steps: 1. NO chlorine bleach. NO heat until clear.
2. Care label check — dry-clean-only / structured / vintage / dyed = pro.
4. OxiClean cool soak 1–6 hr. Check hourly.
5. Carbona / Rit Color Remover for WHITE linen only, ventilate.
6. 3% peroxide for residual yellow on white linen, hidden test on dyed.
7. AIR-DRY plus sun-dry. NO heat until fully clear.
products: Carbona Color Run Remover / Rit Color Remover (white only)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the linen has been hot-dried (often permanent). Stop if it's dyed / structured / vintage / dry-clean-only linen (pro). Stop if Color Remover would strip linen dye. Stop after 2 OxiClean soaks plus Color Remover (if eligible) without progress.
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach — permanent yellow oxidation
NEVER hot water or hot dryer — permanent dye set
Speed matters more than chemistry choice

neverDo: Never chlorine bleach on dye transfer — locks dye permanently
Never hot water or hot dryer until clear
Never Color Remover on dyed linen
Never iron a stained area before clearing
fiberSensitivities: White linen: tolerates OxiClean cool soak, Color Remover, 3% peroxide
Dyed linen: hidden test mandatory before peroxide; Color Remover banned
Structured linen: pro from minute one (warps under wet handling)
Vintage linen: pro from minute one (fiber fragility)
Linen wrinkles aggressively wet — handle gently, no aggressive agitation

materialWarnings: Speed matters more than any product choice
No chlorine bleach — locks the stain permanently
Structured / vintage = pro from minute one

escalation.when: Hot dryer already used (often permanent). Structured / vintage / dyed / dry-clean-only linen. Color Remover ineligible (dyed linen). 2 OxiClean soaks plus eligible Color Remover plateau. Active dye spread continuing during home treatment.
escalation.whatToTell: "Dye transfer on [white / dyed] linen, [fresh / hot-dried], source [denim / red garment / mixed wash], home-treated with [cool re-wash + OxiClean soak × N + Color Remover on white + 3% peroxide on white + sun-dry]. No chlorine bleach, no heat. [Residual color / hot-dried suspected / spreading]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with reducing-bleach chemistry (sodium hydrosulfite controlled dilution) and color-stripping experience

contextQuestions: How fresh — current wash or has it been hot-dried already?
Source of the transfer — denim / red garment / mixed?
Care label — washable, dry-clean-only, structured, vintage?
Has the item been ironed since the transfer?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh + white + not hot-dried = high-confidence home recovery. Hot-dried = often permanent. Dyed linen = sharply narrowed bench (no Color Remover, hidden test on peroxide). Red and navy transfers are the toughest — budget extra OxiClean cycles. Vintage linen is always pro because the fiber doesn't tolerate aggressive home chemistry.
